title: Properties
description: Give your objects characteristics and traits.

slides.title: Adding Traits 🧬
slides.content:
## Properties (Attributes)

Right now, our `User` blueprint is completely empty. We need to define what data every User should possess.

Variables that live inside a class are called **Properties**.

slides.title: Defining Properties 📝
slides.content:
We declare properties using `val` (read-only) or `var` (editable) inside the curly braces of the class.

Every object created from this class will have its own unique copy of these properties.

quizData.question: If you create 100 `Car` objects, how many actual `speed` properties exist in memory?

options.text: Exactly 100. Each Car gets its own independent copy.

quizData.explanation: Every single object spun out of the blueprint holds its very own independent copy of the properties defined within it.

slides.title: Accessing Properties 🔑
slides.content:
To read or change an object's properties later, we use **Dot Notation**. 

Type the variable name of the object, a dot `.`, and then the property name.
slides.codeSnippet:
val car = Car()

// Access properties with the dot (.)
car.color = "Blue" // We changed the paint job!
car.speed = 60

println("My car is ${car.color} and going ${car.speed} mph.")

quizData.question: Which symbol is used to access an internal property contained within an Object?
quizData.explanation: Dot notation is the standard method for reaching 'inside' an object to access its variables and functions.
